segments.title: Bofors and Pom Pom guns answered different air threats
segments.summary: Rather than treating the Bofors as simply superior, Dr. Clarke compares a continuous Pom Pom barrage with a longer-range Bofors response to fewer, more valuable targets.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the simple claim that Sweden developed the Bofors merely to outperform the Pom Pom. He frames both weapons as answers to different expected air threats. A belt-fed Pom Pom suits prolonged encounters with numerous land-based aircraft, where a ship needs sustained fire and can put a dense volume of rounds into the sky. The Bofors has an advantage when fewer aircraft must be engaged more deliberately at somewhat greater range. That makes it especially useful against reconnaissance aircraft whose discovery of a naval force could expose it to later attack. The comparison is therefore about tactical assumptions and target priorities, not a universal ranking of the guns.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts sustained belt-fed Pom Pom fire against mass attacks with Bofors engagements against fewer aircraft, including scouts.
